_patch_meta.session_ref: M38 Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 310 verses across 13 terms

set.analysis_note: The soul actively waits in expectant longing for God's salvation, naming the LORD as its only source of deliverance — an inner posture of hope directed entirely toward divine rescue.

The firstborn sons are ransomed from death through a substitutionary sacrifice, echoing God's covenant-redemption of Israel from Egypt; the inner-being effect is gratitude expressed in ongoing ritual acknowledgment of divine rescue.

The atonement made at priestly ordination is tied to sacred meals; eating the consecrated food seals the inner-being transition from ordinary to atoned-and-set-apart status, with outsiders excluded as uncovered.

Equal ransom payment for every life — rich and poor alike — makes atonement before the LORD, signaling that every soul carries equal standing before God and requires the same covering regardless of status.

The atonement money keeps each Israelite's life in remembrance before God, making atonement an ongoing relational act that brings the person into sustained divine awareness and protection rather than a one-time event.

Moses intercedes as mediator, ascending to God after collective grave sin, with uncertain outcome — the inner-being weight is the people's sin-laden conscience and Moses's sacrificial will to stand between them and divine wrath.

The worshipper's hand-laying on the burnt offering transfers personal identification with the sacrifice, which is then accepted by God to cover the offerer — atonement achieved through substitution, producing the inner state of being accepted.

Priestly atonement through the sin offering results in the definitive inner-being outcome of forgiveness — guilt is objectively removed and the conscience cleared before God by the sacrificial mechanism.

The priest makes atonement specifically for the individual's sin through fat-burning on the altar, and the personal result is forgiveness — the inner being moves from sin-burdened to forgiven through priestly mediation.

Atonement through a pleasing-aroma offering results in personal forgiveness; the aroma metaphor signals that the sacrifice is divinely accepted, and the inner-being outcome is the restoration of right standing before God.

The priest atones for a specific committed sin through a lamb offering, and the sinner is forgiven — the covering is sin-specific and personally directed, resolving individual guilt and restoring the person's standing.

The guilty party brings a specified animal as compensation-offering for a committed sin, and the priest effects atonement — the inner-being movement is from acknowledged guilt through sacrifice to covered status.

A two-bird offering (sin offering plus burnt offering) secures priestly atonement and forgiveness for a committed sin — the paired sacrificial act covers both the sin itself and the offerer's standing before God.

The priest makes atonement and grants forgiveness even for lower-cost grain offerings across a range of offences, showing that atonement coverage extends to the economically vulnerable — no inner-being guilt need remain uncovered.

Atonement for a guilt offering includes restitution plus a fifth added — material repair accompanies ritual covering, signaling that true inner-being forgiveness involves both relational and material restoration.

The priest atones for an unintentional mistake with a guilt-offering ram, and the offender is forgiven — even inadvertent sin creates real guilt before God that requires real atonement and priestly mediation for inner-being resolution.

Priestly atonement before the LORD covers any of the various guilt-producing acts catalogued, resulting in forgiveness — the breadth of covered sins signals comprehensive inner-being relief from accumulated guilt.

The LORD commanded the entire seven-day ordination ritual specifically to make atonement for the priests — the divine mandate frames the atonement as God's own initiative to establish a covered, holy priesthood.

Aaron is commanded to offer atonement for himself first and then for the people — the inner-being sequence is priest-self-covered before mediating for others, establishing that an uncovered mediator cannot cover others.

The sin offering was given to the priests precisely so they could bear and cover the congregation's iniquity before the LORD — the priest's eating enacts the atonement-bearing role, making his participation essential to the people's covered standing.

After childbirth, priestly sacrifice makes atonement and restores the woman's ritual purity — the inner-being movement is from a state of blood-discharge uncleanness to clean, covered, and restored standing before God.

Provision of a lower-cost offering (turtledoves/pigeons) ensures that even the poor mother can receive priestly atonement and ritual cleansing — the inner-being accessibility of covering regardless of economic status is underscored.

Oil applied to the head of the one being cleansed from skin disease is paired with atonement before the LORD — the physical cleansing and priestly atonement work together to restore the person's inner and outer standing.

The sin offering atones specifically for the uncleanness of the person being restored from disease, preceding the burnt offering — atonement addresses the guilt-dimension of the unclean state before the offering of dedication follows.

Burnt and grain offerings complete the atonement sequence for the healed person, with the explicit outcome being cleanness — the inner-being state transitions from diseased-unclean to atoned-clean before God.

A scaled-down offering for the poor person requiring atonement upon healing shows that the atonement mechanism is accessible to all economic levels — the inner-being need for covering is universal regardless of material means.

Oil placed on the head of the one being cleansed is explicitly tied to atonement before the LORD — the anointing gesture marks the moment the person is formally covered and presented as clean before God.

Two affordable birds provide the sin and burnt offerings that enable atonement before the LORD for the economically limited person being cleansed — the outcome of being made clean before God is achievable at any economic level.

Priestly sacrifice of two birds makes atonement for the man with a bodily discharge before the LORD — the ritual covering addresses the ritual impurity-dimension of the bodily condition, restoring relational standing before God.

The priest makes atonement before the LORD for the woman's unclean discharge using two birds — the specifically female bodily uncleanness is covered by the same sacrificial mechanism, establishing equal access to atonement.

The high priest must first make atonement for himself and his household before entering the Holy of Holies — priestly self-covering is the prerequisite for mediating atonement for others, establishing the order of the inner-being covering sequence.

The live scapegoat is presented before the LORD for atonement and then sent away, enacting the removal of sin's burden from the community — the inner-being image is of guilt physically carried away and banished, not merely covered in place.

Aaron must kill the bull as a sin offering and make atonement for himself and his house before proceeding — the high priest's own sin requires covering first, echoing the principle that no uncover mediator can effect others' atonement.

The Holy Place itself must be atoned for because of Israel's accumulated uncleannesses and transgressions — the sanctuary's defilement by proximity to corporate sin is real and requires sacrificial cleansing to restore it as a dwelling for the holy God.

The high priest enters the Holy Place alone to make atonement for himself, his house, and all Israel — his solitary mediation carries the weight of the entire community's standing before God, making this the pinnacle of priestly intercession.

After the inner-sanctuary atonement work, the priest bathes, redresses, and makes the public burnt offerings for himself and the people — the full atonement sequence culminates in a restored, covered priest and a covered people.

The sacrificial animals whose blood effected atonement in the Holy Place must be burned outside the camp — the disposal of the sin-bearing carcasses outside the holy boundary underscores that atonement transfers and removes defilement rather than neutralizing it in place.
The Day of Atonement effects comprehensive cleansing of all sins before the LORD — the inner-being outcome is total cleanness, the conscience unburdened from the full year's accumulated sin-guilt.

The anointed high priest alone, clothed in holy linen, performs the atonement — the garments signal consecrated inner readiness; the act is reserved for the uniquely authorized mediator dressed for the holy work.

The high priest makes atonement comprehensively — for sanctuary, tent, altar, priests, and all the assembly — establishing that the full range of sacred space and persons require covering, with no element of the worshipping community left unaddressed.

Annual atonement for all of Israel's sins is established as a perpetual statute — the recurring inner-being reset of the community before God is divinely mandated, signaling that ongoing sin-accumulation requires a structured, repeatable covering mechanism.
set.keywords: ["atoned", "annual reset", "all sins covered", "corporate covered", "statute perpetual", "community standing restored"]

set.analysis_note: Blood on the altar makes atonement because life is in the blood — God himself gives it as the atonement mechanism for human souls, revealing that the inner-being covering of the soul requires the substitution of life for life.

A guilt-offering ram secures priestly atonement and forgiveness for a specific sexual sin before the LORD — even this morally grave transgression is covered through the proper sacrificial mechanism, with forgiveness explicitly stated as the outcome.

The Day of Atonement requires full rest from work because its exclusive purpose is making atonement for the entire people before God — inner-being attention is wholly directed to the divinely-appointed covering event.

When no kinsman-redeemer exists to receive restitution, the atonement ram still covers the wrongdoer before the LORD — the mechanism for inner-being guilt-resolution is preserved even when the human relational channel is absent.

Accidental contact with a corpse breaks a Nazirite's vow and creates real sin-guilt requiring sacrificial atonement before the head can be reconsecrated — even unintended defilement generates an inner-being guilt-state that demands priestly covering.

Sacrificial atonement is made specifically for the Levites at their consecration — the entire tribe requires priestly covering before taking up their sanctuary service, establishing that ministry to God requires atoned-for status.

The Levites are given to serve and make atonement for Israel so that divine plague does not strike when the people draw near the sanctuary — their atoning service is a protection of Israel's inner-being safety before a holy God.

Aaron makes atonement for the purified and washed Levites, completing their cleansing — the self-purification by the Levites is necessary but insufficient; priestly atonement is the final act that achieves their clean standing before God.

The priest makes atonement for the entire congregation's unintentional sin, and they are forgiven — corporate inadvertent transgression is covered through collective sacrifice and priestly mediation, with forgiveness explicitly granted to the whole community.
The priest's ritual act covers the unintentional sinner before God, resulting in forgiveness — the inner being moves from guilt-state to the state of being forgiven through mediated atonement.
Atonement is urgently needed to halt active divine wrath manifesting as plague; the inner-being stakes are survival and the averting of God's wrath through priestly intercession.
Aaron's swift priestly action with incense makes atonement and arrests the plague, showing the inner-being community standing between wrath and safety through mediated covering.
Zealous priestly action makes atonement for corporate sin, securing a perpetual covenant reward; the inner-being register is zeal motivating atoning action on behalf of a people.
A sin-offering goat is prescribed specifically to make atonement for the people, indicating the ritual mechanism by which guilt is covered before God.
A male goat sin offering is appointed to make atonement, providing the scheduled covering of guilt for the community before God.
A sin-offering goat is prescribed to make atonement, furnishing the ritual means by which the community's guilt is covered at the appointed feast.
Warriors bring gold offerings voluntarily to make atonement before God, indicating a personal inner-being response of acknowledgment and self-offering to secure standing before the Lord.

Blood guilt from murder pollutes the land and admits no atonement except by the killer's own blood; no ritual covering suffices — the inner-being implication is that intentional bloodshed places guilt beyond standard atonement.
God's redemption of his people from slavery is grounded in his love and covenant oath, not their merit; the inner being is rescued by divine initiative alone.
Moses intercedes appealing to God's past redemption of the people as grounds for mercy; the inner-being register is pleading trust grounded in remembered deliverance.
The Exodus redemption is cited as the basis for loyalty to God; rebellion against the Redeemer constitutes an aggravated betrayal, making redemption the inner-being standard for allegiance.
Memory of God's past redemption from slavery grounds the command to treat slaves generously; the inner being is motivated by gratitude for having been ransomed.
The prayer invokes both atonement for blood guilt and Israel's status as a redeemed people, seeking the removal of corporate guilt from the community's conscience before God.
Israel's identity as the redeemed people is invoked as the basis for appealing for atonement of blood guilt; redemption establishes relational standing before God.
Memory of redemption from Egypt is commanded as the motivating inner-being basis for just treatment of the vulnerable; past ransom shapes present ethical will.

God pronounces that the iniquity of the priestly house cannot be atoned for by sacrifice or offering ever — the inner-being weight is the finality of unatoned guilt when persistent sin has closed off the atonement-pathway.
The people ransom Jonathan from a death sentence, rescuing the one God used to bring salvation; the inner being of the community acts in solidarity to preserve life.
David appeals to God as the one who has personally redeemed his life from all adversity, expressing a soul-level trust grounded in experienced deliverance.
Israel is the unique nation God himself went out to redeem, establishing his name by awesome deeds; redemption defines the people's identity and the relational bond with their God.
David seeks to know what act of atonement will satisfy the Gibeonites and restore blessing to the land; the inner-being register is guilt-seeking-resolution in a relational repair context.
set.keywords: ["atoned", "guilt seeking resolution", "relational repair", "mediation seeking", "blood guilt", "will turned toward reconciliation"]

set.analysis_note: Solomon's father David swears by God who has redeemed his soul from every adversity; the soul's repeated rescue is the experiential ground of personal trust.
Aaron and his sons' ongoing sacrificial ministry makes atonement for all Israel as its comprehensive purpose; the priestly vocation is the mediation of corporate atonement-standing before God.
set.keywords: ["atoned", "mediation priestly", "corporate guilt covered", "intercession ongoing", "worship ordered", "sin offering"]

set.analysis_note: God's act of going to redeem Israel as his own people is the defining event that established his name; redemption constitutes the people's identity and his glory.
Priests make comprehensive atonement for all Israel through sin-offering blood on the altar, restoring corporate standing before God after a period of neglected worship.
set.keywords: ["atoned", "corporate guilt covered", "mediation priestly", "sin offering", "restoration worship", "guilt removed"]

set.analysis_note: Hezekiah prays for God to pardon those ritually unprepared who sought him anyway; the inner-being register is royal intercession appealing to God's goodness to cover sincere but irregular seekers.
The returning exiles are identified as God's servants whom he redeemed by his great power; redemption is the basis for the intercessor's appeal for God's continued favour.
The community commits to sustain the sin offerings that make atonement for Israel as part of covenant renewal, showing corporate responsibility for maintaining the atonement system.
God's ransoming activity is presented as delivering the person from mortal dangers — famine and war — with the soul rescued from death through divine protection.
Job frames ransom as deliverance from a threatening adversary; the inner-being register is the soul's cry for rescue from oppressive danger through divine intervention.
Job claims that his willingness to approach God despite danger will itself be his salvation, as only the godless cannot stand before God; confidence in one's integrity becomes the ground of hope.
set.keywords: ["salvation present", "hope sustained", "confidence before god", "integrity trusted", "heart trusting", "perishing averted"]

set.analysis_note: God's ransom of the soul from the pit results in restored life and light; the inner being moves from death-facing darkness to life and sight through divine redemption.
The psalmist pleads for corporate ransom of God's people from all their troubles; the inner-being register is communal distress calling out for divine redemptive intervention.
The psalmist pairs a commitment to walk in integrity with a plea for personal ransom and grace; redemption and moral faithfulness are linked in the inner-being posture.
The psalmist commits his spirit into God's hands, grounding trust in past redemption; the soul's total self-surrender rests on experienced ransom by the faithful God.
God redeems the lives of his servants and none who trust in him face condemnation; the inner being moves from endangered to safe through redemptive divine care.
The community urgently pleads for redemption appealing to God's steadfast love, crying from a place of shame and defeat; the soul waits in hope for divine rescue.
No human can ransom another person's life from death — the price is beyond any human ability; the inner-being register is the limit of human power over ultimate mortality.
God alone will ransom the psalmist's soul from Sheol and receive him; the inner being rests in eschatological confidence that God overcomes death where no human can.
God redeems the psalmist's soul safely from battle against many enemies; the inner being experiences rescue and preservation through divine intervention amid external threat.
When iniquities overwhelm the psalmist, God himself atones for the transgressions; the inner being is relieved of a burden it cannot bear by God's covering action.
The psalmist pleads for God to draw near and ransom the soul from enemies; the inner being is in distress, calling for divine proximity and redemptive rescue.
The redeemed soul's response is overflowing joy in song and praise; redemption produces inner-being exultation expressed through lips and soul together.
God's compassion led him to atone for his people's iniquity and restrain his wrath repeatedly rather than destroying them; the inner-being weight is mercy overcoming deserved judgment.
The people forgot the day God redeemed them from their enemy; the inner being failed to hold the memory of past deliverance, leading to ingratitude and faithlessness.
The plea asks God to atone for sins for his name's sake alongside deliverance, linking the community's sin-covered state directly with the need for both rescue and forgiveness.
The psalmist asks to be redeemed from human oppression so that obedience to God's precepts becomes possible; ransom here enables the will's free response to God.
God will ransom Israel from all its iniquities — a comprehensive eschatological redemption that covers every sin; the soul's deepest hope is total redemption from sin's hold.
Iniquity is atoned for through steadfast love and faithfulness, and the fear of the Lord turns one from evil; atonement and moral transformation are joined as inner-being outcomes.
Zion's redemption comes through justice and righteousness; those who repent receive the ransom — the inner being must turn in repentance for the redemptive rescue to take effect.
The burning coal's touch enacts atonement on Isaiah's lips — guilt is removed and sin covered, cleansing the conscience so the prophet stands before God without condemnation.
God himself is named as the speaker's salvation, replacing fear with trust and strength, so the inner being rests securely in present experienced deliverance.
Salvation is pictured as a well from which the redeemed draw with joy, evoking an inner-being posture of glad, active reception of God's delivered life.
God declares that this particular iniquity will not be atoned for in the people's lifetime — atonement is withheld, leaving guilt unresolved and the inner person facing judgment unrelieved.
The redeemed community's patient waiting is vindicated when salvation arrives; they respond with glad rejoicing, showing that salvation transforms the soul from waiting tension to exultant joy.
set.keywords: ["hope sustained", "salvation arrived", "joy salvation", "waiting vindicated", "soul rejoicing", "god saving"]

set.analysis_note: Salvation is figured as the city's defensive walls and bulwarks — an outer security that corresponds to inner stability and confidence granted to those inside.
Human effort at deliverance has failed completely — the people labored but produced nothing, highlighting the impossibility of self-achieved salvation and the despair that results.
Guilt will be atoned through the removal of idolatry — the people's sin is covered only when false worship is completely destroyed, linking atonement to genuine inner and cultic turning.
God who ransomed Abraham now promises liberation for Jacob's descendants — past redemption grounds present promise, replacing shame and pallor with restored dignity.
The community pleads for salvation in a time of distress, placing their waiting trust entirely in God as their only arm and deliverer, expressing the soul's desperate reliance.
Salvation is listed alongside wisdom and knowledge as an enduring abundance characterizing the stable era God brings — it is a lasting inner-being treasure, not a momentary rescue.
Those ransomed by the Lord return with everlasting joy crowning them — ransom transforms inner being from sorrow and sighing to gladness, marking the ransomed state as one of sustained rejoicing.
The proud city is told no charm or act can atone for the coming disaster — atonement is unavailable to the defiant, leaving the inner being exposed and helpless before judgment.
God's salvation is extended through the servant to all nations to the ends of the earth — the inner-being scope is universal inclusion in deliverance, not merely national restoration.
God answers and helps in a divinely appointed day of salvation, sustaining the servant as a covenant mediator — salvation is experienced as divine favor and active support in the appointed moment.
Contrasted with a perishing cosmos, God's salvation is declared everlasting — the soul anchors eternal hope in a salvation that outlasts all created things, never to be dismayed.
Enemies' power decays like moth-eaten cloth, but God's salvation endures to all generations — the inner being finds permanent security in salvation that time cannot erode.
The ransomed of the Lord return to Zion crowned with everlasting joy — ransom issues in the fullest inner-being transformation: sorrow and sighing utterly replaced by gladness.
The messenger proclaims salvation as beautiful good news — the inner-being effect is the joyful reception of the announcement that God reigns and salvation has arrived.
God's revealed arm brings salvation visible to all nations — the universal display evokes awe and confidence as all ends of the earth witness God's saving power.
The nearness of coming salvation motivates righteous conduct — the inner being is oriented toward imminent deliverance, aligning the will with justice in anticipation.
The community mourns that salvation is far off — the inner being groans in unfulfilled longing, experiencing the anguish of a salvation hoped for but not yet received.
God arms himself with a helmet of salvation as divine warrior — salvation is the protective covering he wears in battle, assuring the delivered that his saving intent is militant and certain.
The redeemed city's very walls are renamed Salvation — the inner-being identity of the community is wholly defined by the encompassing security of God's saving presence.
God declares he will not rest until Jerusalem's salvation blazes forth — the divine will is urgently bent toward the people's full deliverance, assuring them salvation will not be withheld.
God personally promises to deliver and ransom the prophet from violent oppressors — the redemption is concrete and relational, replacing endangerment with divine protection.
The prophet intercedes in reverse — pleading that the enemies' iniquity NOT be atoned or covered, invoking divine wrath rather than forgiveness, highlighting atonement's absence as judgment.
God has ransomed his people from overpowering enemies — the ransom is an accomplished act of covenant rescue, freeing the inner being from subjugation to a force beyond human strength.

God's act of atonement for all the people's past sins produces deep shame and silenced contrition in them — inner-being response to being atoned-for is humbled, wordless gratitude mixed with remembered guilt.
Sacrifice from the flock provides the material means of atonement for the people — the inner-being state addressed is one of ongoing need for covering, met through prescribed offering.

The prince bears responsibility to provide all appointed sacrifices for national atonement — the whole community's inner-being status before God is kept covered through regularized, mediated offering.

The seventy-weeks decree includes atoning for iniquity as one of six eschatological goals — atonement is presented as a cosmic, once-for-all act that brings everlasting righteousness in place of accumulated sin.

God declares willingness to ransom but is blocked by the people's deceptive rebellion — ransom is offered but refused by a will turned away, so the inner being remains in self-imposed spiritual danger.
God announces ransom from Sheol and redemption from Death itself — the inner being faces ultimate annihilation, and God's saving act reaches to the deepest existential threat possible.
God recalls the Exodus redemption from slavery as covenant ground for present obligation — past ransom shapes the inner being's memory and gratitude, forming the basis of covenantal response.
God rides a chariot of salvation into cosmic battle — salvation is the mode of his warrior advance, assuring the inner being that divine power is actively deployed for rescue.
set.keywords: ["salvation eschatological", "god saving", "divine warrior", "rescue active", "power divine", "deliverance certain"]

set.analysis_note: God whistles to gather the scattered because he has redeemed them — accomplished redemption is the ground of regathering, restoring the people to fullness and belonging.

Jesus is named and defined by his mission: to save his people from their sins — the salvation is eschatological/spiritual rescue, targeting the inner person's bondage to sin as the structural opposite.
set.keywords: ["salvation eschatological", "sin removed", "rescue spiritual", "christ saving", "people delivered", "name defined"]

set.analysis_note: The disciples cry 'Save us' in mortal danger of drowning — this is physical rescue from perishing, with the inner being gripped by terror and crying out in urgent, desperate faith.
The woman's inner faith-speech — 'if I touch his garment I will be made well' — shows sōzō in the sense of physical healing with spiritual overtone, driven by quiet interior conviction.
Jesus confirms healing through faith — 'your faith has made you well' — the sōzō is healing with spiritual resonance, with faith as the inner-being faculty that activates the saving act.
set.keywords: ["healing spiritual", "faith exercised", "wholeness received", "heart trusting", "salvation present", "christ saving"]

set.analysis_note: Eschatological salvation belongs to the one who endures to the end — the inner will must hold under hatred and persecution; salvation is the future state secured by persevering faith.
Peter, sinking in wind and waves, cries 'Lord, save me' — physical rescue from drowning, with the inner being moving from bold venture to panic and desperate faith-cry in an instant.
Saving one's life by self-preservation leads to loss; losing it for Christ's sake leads to finding it — sōzō here is eschatological self-realization, requiring the will to surrender self-interest.
set.keywords: ["salvation eschatological", "self surrendered", "will turning", "life found", "loss transformed", "christ saving"]

set.analysis_note: The disciples are astonished asking who can be saved — eschatological salvation seems impossibly out of reach by human effort, driving the question to God's power as the only ground.
Eschatological salvation comes to the one who endures to the end amid tribulation — the inner faculty of perseverance is the decisive characteristic; salvation is the telos of sustained endurance.
If the tribulation days were not cut short no flesh would survive — sōzō here carries physical preservation merged with eschatological rescue, both conditional on divine intervention for the elect.
Mockers taunt Jesus to save himself by descending the cross — the irony is that physical self-rescue is possible but chosen against, as his refusal is the very means of others' salvation.
Mockers note he saved others but cannot save himself — the taunt ironically witnesses to both his past healings/deliverances and the self-emptying that makes eschatological salvation possible.
set.keywords: ["rescue physical", "salvation eschatological", "self emptied", "christ saving", "irony salvation", "others delivered"]

set.analysis_note: Bystanders speculate whether Elijah will come to rescue Jesus — physical rescue from the cross in view, though the expectation is unfulfilled, underscoring that no human deliverance interrupts the appointed suffering.

A father's desperate plea for physical healing of his dying daughter — sōzō here names rescue from imminent death, with the inner being engaged through urgent, earnest faith and parental love seeking life.

The hemorrhaging woman's inner conviction that mere contact with Jesus will restore her — sōzō is physical healing-with-spiritual-overtone, driven by faith working in the heart before any word is spoken.

Jesus declares the woman's faith the operative cause of her healing and grants her peace — sōzō bridges physical restoration and spiritual wholeness, with faith named as the inner faculty that activated rescue.

Jesus inverts the instinct of self-preservation: clinging to life for oneself leads to loss, but surrendering life for the gospel's sake saves it — sōzō here is eschatological salvation of the true self through self-renunciation.

The disciples' astonishment at wealth's barrier to salvation surfaces the question of eschatological rescue as humanly impossible — sōzō names final deliverance, and the inner state is one of alarmed helplessness apart from God.

Jesus credits Bartimaeus's faith with his sight-restoration — sōzō is physical-healing-with-spiritual-overtone, and the immediate following of Jesus signals that wholeness of person, not just eyes, was given.

Endurance through hatred and persecution to the end is the condition tied to eschatological salvation — sōzō names final deliverance, and the inner faculty required is steadfast perseverance of will against external pressure.

God's sovereign shortening of tribulation days is the condition for any human surviving — sōzō here names preservation of physical life within eschatological rescue, with the elect as its object and divine election as its ground.

Mockers taunt the crucified Jesus to rescue himself from physical death — sōzō is used in the ironic sense of self-preservation, with no genuine faith content; the inner-being irony is that true salvation requires not coming down.

The priests' mockery — 'he saved others; he cannot save himself' — ironically testifies to Jesus's prior saving acts while denying his power; sōzō oscillates between physical rescue and the deeper saving mission he will not abandon.

Belief and baptism yield eschatological salvation; unbelief yields condemnation — sōzō names final rescue from judgment, with faith as the decisive inner faculty and condemnation as the structural opposite.

Mary's spirit exults in God as her Savior — the inner being (spirit) is engaged in joyful, worshipful response to divine rescue, naming God as the personal source of salvation experienced in the soul.

The raising of a horn of salvation in David's house declares a corporate, covenantal rescue — soteria names mighty deliverance coming through the Davidic line, evoking communal hope and the fulfillment of ancestral promise.
Salvation is defined here as rescue from enemies and those who hate — soteria names deliverance from hostile threat, engaging a corporate sense of safety and vindication for a besieged people.

Salvation-knowledge is given to God's people through forgiveness of sins — soteria here links cognitive reception of the saving message with the inner state of having sins forgiven, uniting proclamation and conscience-cleansing.

The angelic birth announcement names the newborn as Savior, Christ, and Lord — sōtēr identifies the person of Jesus as the singular source of salvation, grounding hope in a historical event with eschatological scope.

Simeon sees the child and declares his eyes have witnessed God's salvation — sōtērion is salvation made visible and personally witnessed, producing a readiness to depart in peace as one whose hope is fulfilled.

The prophetic vision that all flesh shall see God's salvation — sōtērion here is universal saving disclosure, engaging corporate hope for a salvation that transcends ethnic boundaries and becomes visible to all humanity.

Jesus tells the sinful woman her faith has saved her — sōzō here is spiritual/eschatological salvation through faith, including forgiveness, with peace as the inner-being state conferred upon one previously burdened by sin.

The devil removes the word from hearts to prevent belief and salvation — sōzō is eschatological salvation contingent on faith, with the heart as the battleground where the word must take root to produce rescue.

Jesus attributes the healing of the hemorrhaging woman to her faith and sends her away in peace — sōzō names physical-healing-with-spiritual-overtone, peace being the inner-being state that seals the wholeness given.

Jesus counters fear with the command to believe so that the girl will be made well — sōzō names physical rescue from death, with faith explicitly set against fear as the inner faculty that opens the door to saving action.

Self-preservation of life for its own sake leads to eschatological loss; surrendering life for Jesus's sake saves it — sōzō names ultimate redemption of the true self through the will's willingness to let go.
The question of how many will be saved prompts Jesus's warning to strive for entry — sōzō here is eschatological salvation presented as narrow and requiring active response, not passive assumption.

Jesus tells the returning Samaritan leper that his faith has made him well — sōzō carries healing-with-spiritual-overtone, with faith distinguished as the inner faculty that brings wholeness beyond the merely physical.

Seeking to preserve one's life results in losing it; losing it results in keeping it — sōzō names eschatological preservation through surrender, requiring the will to release self-protective clinging.

The tax collector's prayer 'be merciful to me, a sinner' — hilaskomai here is a plea for propitiatory mercy from one who stands at distance, confessing unworthiness; the inner being is crushed, sin-aware, and wholly dependent on divine mercy.

The crowd's stunned question — who then can be saved? — frames eschatological salvation as humanly impossible, driving the inner being toward complete reliance on God rather than human resources or merit.
Jesus grants sight to the blind man and attributes it to his faith — sōzō is physical-healing-with-spiritual-overtone, and faith is again the named inner faculty through which wholeness comes.

Jesus declares salvation has come to Zacchaeus's house today — soteria names present, personal, household-encompassing rescue arriving through the presence and word of Jesus, grounded in covenant identity.

The Son of Man's mission is to seek and save the lost — sōzō defines eschatological rescue as directed toward those who are perished/strayed, with Christ as the active seeker and savior of those outside.

Rulers mock Jesus: he saved others but cannot save himself — sōzō is used sarcastically of physical self-rescue, while ironically testifying to the saving acts for others that constitute his true mission.
Soldiers mock Jesus to save himself if he is king — sōzō is taunted physical self-rescue, with no faith content; the irony is that his refusal to save himself enacts salvation for others.
A criminal demands Jesus save himself and them — sōzō is physical deliverance from crucifixion, demanded without repentance or faith; it contrasts with the other criminal's penitent trust.

God sent his Son not to condemn but to save the world — sōzō is universal eschatological salvation as the positive divine purpose, with condemnation as the structural opposite that the mission displaces.

Jesus speaks of the gift of God as living water — dōrea here names an unrecognized divine gift whose reception requires recognizing the giver; the inner-being state is potential transformation from spiritual thirst to living sustenance.

Salvation is declared to originate from the Jews — soteria here names the covenantal-historical source of redemption, grounding saving knowledge in particular revelation rather than generic religious seeking.

The Samaritans confess Jesus as Savior of the world based on direct encounter — sōtēr names universal saving identity recognized by personal hearing and faith, expanding beyond ethnicity to all humanity.

Jesus says he mentions human testimony so that hearers may be saved — sōzō is eschatological salvation as the goal of his words, with the will to believe as the required inner response to the testimony offered.

Jesus as the door promises that whoever enters through him will be saved and find pasture — sōzō names full eschatological security and flourishing, with Christ as the exclusive means of access and safety.

Jesus's soul is troubled and he contemplates asking the Father to save him from this hour — sōzō is physical rescue from suffering/death, briefly considered but surrendered; the inner being submits its anguish to redemptive purpose.

Jesus did not come to judge but to save the world — sōzō again states the positive saving mission as the purpose of the incarnation, with judgment as the structural opposite that is displaced by saving intent.

Everyone who calls on the Lord's name will be saved — sōzō is eschatological salvation available through the inner act of calling/invoking, with the name as the exclusive ground and calling as the responsive will.

The gift of the Holy Spirit is promised to all who repent and are baptized — dōrea names the Spirit as the salvation-bearing divine gift given upon repentance, linking the inner turning of will with the reception of divine presence.

Peter exhorts the crowd to save themselves from a corrupt generation — sōzō is eschatological-moral rescue requiring a decisive break with surrounding sinfulness, engaging the will to separate and align with God's people.

Peter's call to 'save yourselves from this crooked generation' names eschatological-moral rescue as requiring personal response — the will must actively break with corrupt surroundings to enter the saved community.

The Lord day by day added to the community those being saved — sōzō is ongoing eschatological salvation as communal incorporation, with God as the active agent adding persons into the body of the rescued.

Salvation is in no one else — Jesus's name is the sole means of eschatological rescue given to humanity; the inner implication is that trust must be concentrated exclusively in him with no alternative route to safety.

Salvation belongs to no one else — soteria here is the exclusive possession tied to one name, named alongside the claim that no other name saves; the inner-being state required is exclusive faith-trust in Christ alone.

God exalted Jesus as Leader and Savior to give repentance and forgiveness — sōtēr here names Christ's exalted office through which inner transformation (repentance) and relational restoration (forgiveness) flow to those he saves.

God was actively working deliverance through a chosen hand, but the inner faculty of understanding was closed — the people failed to perceive the salvation being offered, showing that rescue requires both divine initiative and receptive comprehension.

The gift of God is by nature freely given and cannot be purchased; the attempt to buy it reveals a heart that has misunderstood grace as a commodity, and Peter's curse names the spiritual danger of that inner orientation.

The Spirit poured out as free gift on Gentiles astonishes Jewish believers, revealing that God's saving gift transcends ethnic boundary — the inner response is one of amazement at grace received by the unexpected.

Salvation is conveyed through a proclaimed message received by an entire household, linking eschatological rescue to hearing and communal response — the whole inner-being and social unit is the target of saving.

God gave the same Spirit-gift to Gentile believers as to Jewish believers upon faith, and this equal gifting prevents human resistance to God's saving purpose — the inner logic is that the gift itself defines the boundary of belonging.

God fulfills his covenant promise to Israel by raising up Jesus as Savior from David's line, grounding eschatological rescue in historical promise and divine faithfulness — the savior is the object of inner hope now realized.

The message of salvation has been sent specifically to those who fear God and belong to Abraham's heritage — it is a word dispatched to receptive hearers, positioning salvation as something to be received through proclaimed truth.

By divine command, salvation is to be brought to the ends of the earth through those made lights to the Gentiles — salvation is here a universal eschatological reality carried by human agents under divine mandate.

A false teaching insists circumcision is necessary for salvation, placing an external ritual condition on eschatological rescue — the verse surfaces the inner tension between grace-reception and works-requirement for being saved.
set.keywords: ["salvation eschatological", "works demanded", "grace contested", "will pressured", "rescue conditional false", "faith undermined"]

set.analysis_note: Salvation comes through grace of the Lord Jesus equally for all, not through law-observance — the inner faculty of believing receives grace, and that grace is the sole operative means of eschatological rescue.

Salvation is described as a way — a path to be proclaimed and entered — and its messengers are identified as servants of the Most High God, framing salvation as both a disclosed route and a divine commission.

The jailer's urgent question — what must I do to be saved — reveals a conscience awakened to danger and a will actively seeking rescue, making this a prime instance of the inner being turning toward salvation.

Salvation is directly tied to believing in the Lord Jesus — the inner act of faith is the condition, and the promise extends to the household, so that rescue is eschatological and personally appropriated through trust.

In the context of a life-threatening storm, all hope of physical rescue was abandoned by those aboard — this is sōzō in its physical/temporal rescue sense, with the inner state being one of despair and lost hope.

God's salvation has been sent to the Gentiles who will listen — the receptive inner posture of hearing contrasts with those who rejected it, and sōtērion names God's saving purpose now redirected to a new audience.

The gospel is God's power unto salvation for everyone who believes — the inner act of faith is the reception point, and salvation is the outcome of that divine power meeting human trust, without shame or hesitation.

Christ is set forth by God as the propitiation through his blood, received by faith — the inner-being effect is that wrath is averted and former sins are passed over, establishing the foundational mechanism of atonement.

Having been justified by Christ's blood, believers are assured of future salvation from God's wrath — the inner state moves from justified-now to saved-from-wrath-future, establishing an eschatological security grounded in past atonement.

Reconciliation through Christ's death guarantees ongoing salvation through his risen life — the inner-being moves from enemy-status through reconciliation to present-and-future rescue, making salvation a relational restoration.

The free gift of grace through Christ far exceeds the trespass of Adam in scope and abundance — grace abounds for many where death spread, so the inner-being effect is overflowing gift received against the backdrop of condemnation.

Unlike the single trespass that brought condemnation, the free gift following many trespasses brings justification — the inner-being state moves from guilt-accumulated to justified-by-gift, with the gift's magnitude matching the extent of sin.
set.keywords: ["justification received", "gift reversing", "condemnation overcome", "guilt covered", "sin multiple", "grace exceeding"]

set.analysis_note: Those who receive the abundance of grace and the gift of righteousness will reign in life — the inner-being transforms from death-dominated to life-reigning through received righteousness, a royal inner state bestowed by gift.
set.keywords: ["righteousness received", "grace abundant", "life reigning", "gift empowering", "death overcome", "inner transformation"]

set.analysis_note: Believers were saved in hope — salvation is already possessed but not yet fully visible, so the inner-being lives in a present salvation sustained by forward-looking hope, not by sight of completed rescue.

Despite Israel's vast numbers, only a remnant will be saved — eschatological rescue is not guaranteed by ethnic belonging but by divine election, so the inner-being stands before the narrowing of salvation to the chosen few.

The apostle's heart-desire and intercessory prayer is entirely oriented toward the salvation of his kinspeople — naming the inner faculties of desire and prayer as the instruments through which one seeks another's rescue.
set.keywords: ["heart desiring", "prayer intercessory", "salvation sought", "salvation eschatological", "love motivating", "will engaged"]

set.analysis_note: Salvation comes through confessing Christ as Lord with the mouth and believing in the heart that God raised him — the inner faculties of heart-faith and verbal confession together constitute the way of being saved.

Heart-belief leads to justification while mouth-confession leads to salvation — salvation is the full outer expression of an inner faith, with heart and mouth as the dual inner-being faculties through which rescue is appropriated.

Salvation is universally available to everyone who calls on the Lord's name — the inner act of calling is the condition, with no restriction on person, framing rescue as entirely accessible through earnest invocation.

Israel's trespass became the occasion for salvation reaching the Gentiles, provoking jealousy — salvation here is a dynamic historical movement that uses stumbling as a channel of grace, not a permanent fall into ruin.

The apostle's personal missionary strategy aims to provoke jealousy among his kinspeople in order to save some of them — salvation is the relational goal of one person's witness toward others, person-to-person in direction.
set.keywords: ["salvation sought", "witness motivating", "person saving", "jealousy provoking", "love missional", "will striving"]

set.analysis_note: All Israel will ultimately be saved when the Deliverer comes from Zion to banish ungodliness — this is eschatological national rescue tied to prophetic promise, the inner-being of a people rescued from ungodliness collectively.

Salvation is temporally nearer than when believers first believed, calling them to wake from spiritual sleep — the inner-being is addressed in terms of eschatological urgency, with salvation as an approaching horizon that demands alertness.
set.keywords: ["salvation eschatological", "urgency inner", "awakening called", "hope nearer", "will roused", "salvation present"]

set.analysis_note: The word of the cross is power to those being saved but folly to those perishing — the inner state of being-saved versus perishing is an active present divide, with the cross as the operative power of ongoing rescue.

God chose the foolishness of preaching to save those who believe — human wisdom failed to reach God, so God bypassed it with proclamation, making faith-in-message the inner pathway to eschatological rescue.

Though a person's work is consumed, the person themselves will be saved — eschatological rescue of the self remains even when labor is lost, distinguishing the salvation of the person from the value of their works.

Delivering a man to Satan for fleshly destruction aims at the ultimate salvation of his spirit on the day of the Lord — the body is sacrificed so the inner spirit may be rescued eschatologically, revealing a radical spiritual priority.
set.keywords: ["spirit saved", "flesh destroyed", "salvation eschatological", "discipline redemptive", "inner preserved", "judgment anticipated"]

set.analysis_note: A believing spouse holds uncertain hope of saving their unbelieving partner — eschatological rescue is possible through relational witness, but the outcome remains unknown, naming the hope without guaranteeing it.
set.keywords: ["salvation uncertain", "witness relational", "hope sustained", "person saving", "rescue possible", "will unknown"]

set.analysis_note: Apostolic self-adaptation to all people aims at saving some — missional flexibility of the will and person is wholly driven by the eschatological goal of rescue, with the apostle's inner identity shaped by that saving purpose.

Self-denial in all things is motivated by the desire that many may be saved — the inner faculty of other-oriented will, refusing personal advantage, serves as the means through which eschatological rescue reaches others.

The gospel by which believers are being saved requires holding fast — salvation is a present ongoing state contingent on continued faith, with the warning of believing in vain naming the risk of inner defection.
set.keywords: ["salvation present", "rescued ongoing", "faith holding", "will persisting", "defection warned", "gospel received"]

set.analysis_note: The apostle's afflictions work for the salvation and comfort of others who endure the same sufferings — salvation is experienced through patient endurance of suffering, linking inner steadfastness to the salvific process.
set.keywords: ["salvation through suffering", "endurance inner", "comfort received", "salvation present", "solidarity salvific", "patience salvation"]

set.analysis_note: Believers are an aroma of Christ to God — the present-tense divide between those being saved and those perishing is a living spiritual reality, with the same witness producing opposite inner outcomes in different hearers.

The day of salvation is declared present now — the inner-being is urged to receive God's help in this very moment, with eschatological rescue made an urgent present reality, not a distant future deferral.

Godly grief produces repentance that leads to salvation without regret, contrasting with worldly grief that leads to death — salvation is reached through a specific inner journey: godly sorrow, genuine turning, and life-giving outcome.
set.keywords: ["repentance leading", "salvation eschatological", "grief godly", "will turning", "sorrow productive", "death contrasted"]

set.analysis_note: Paul bursts into doxology over God's inexpressible gift — the inner response of gratitude and praise overflows at the gift's immeasurable character, though the specific gift is unnamed, the emotional-worship response is the focus.

Hearing the gospel of salvation and believing leads to being sealed with the Holy Spirit — the inner sequence is: word heard, faith exercised, Spirit sealed, confirming eschatological rescue as a Spirit-marked inner reality.
set.keywords: ["gospel salvation", "faith exercised", "spirit sealed", "salvation eschatological", "hearing faith", "inner confirmed"]

set.analysis_note: Even while spiritually dead in trespasses, believers were made alive with Christ and saved by grace — grace acts on an inner state of death to produce salvation, with no inner contribution from the dead self.

Salvation by grace through faith is entirely God's gift, not human achievement — the inner act of faith receives what grace gives, but neither faith nor salvation originates in the self, securing complete reliance on God.

Christ is Savior of the church as its head — the metaphor locates saving in the relational headship of Christ over his body, framing salvation as ongoing protective headship rather than a single past act.

The helmet of salvation is worn as protective armor in spiritual warfare — salvation is an inner reality that shields the mind and identity of the believer against spiritual assault, a possession worn for defensive protection.
set.keywords: ["salvation protecting", "mind shielded", "rescue possessed", "spiritual warfare", "safety worn", "identity secured"]

set.analysis_note: Paul expects his imprisonment to result in personal deliverance through corporate prayer and the Spirit's aid, so salvation here names a present-experience rescue of the person from crisis, with the inner being sustained by hope.
The believers' courageous, unflinching stance before opponents is itself a sign that God is the source of their salvation, contrasting their destiny of rescue with the opponents' destruction.
Believers are urged to actively work out their own salvation with reverent seriousness, indicating that salvation is a present ongoing state engaging the will, conscience, and deep moral seriousness.

The inner being is oriented in hopeful anticipation toward the Savior returning from heaven, so the soul's citizenship and expectation are anchored eschatologically rather than in present earthly existence.

Opponents block the Gentiles from hearing the gospel that would bring eschatological salvation; the contrast between being saved and wrath coming upon the hinderers marks the stakes of the rescue rejected.
Salvation is pictured as a helmet protecting the mind and will, with hope of salvation arming the believer for sobriety and vigilance; the inner faculty engaged is sustained eschatological hope.
God's sovereign appointment contrasts wrath with obtaining salvation, so the inner being rests in divine election that removes the dread of condemnation and replaces it with assured rescue.
Those perishing refused love of truth and therefore forfeited salvation; the inner-being faculty at stake is the will's deliberate rejection of truth that would have brought rescue.
Salvation is received through the Spirit's sanctifying work and personal belief in truth, located in the inner transformation of mind and heart chosen by God from the beginning.
Christ came specifically to save sinners, and the speaker's self-identification as chief sinner intensifies the personal reach of this rescue — salvation penetrates to the most guilty conscience.
God desires all people to be saved and come to know the truth, so salvation is linked to the inner mind's reception of truth — rescue and knowledge are joined as divine intent for every person.
Salvation is promised through faithfulness in a woman's domestic sphere, conditioned on persisting in faith, love, holiness, and self-control — salvation sustained through ongoing inner virtue.
Hope is set on the living God as Savior of all people, especially believers; the inner faculty engaged is hope actively anchoring the person in God's universal saving will.
A teacher who watches his life and doctrine carefully will save both himself and his hearers; salvation here is eschatological rescue mediated through faithful teaching engaging conscience and will.

God saved believers and called them to holiness by grace purposed before time, not by works; the inner being is rescued by unearned divine initiative, producing identity rooted in grace rather than achievement.
The Savior's appearing abolished death and brought immortality to light through the gospel; the inner being is addressed at the deepest level — mortality's fear displaced by life revealed.

Paul endures suffering so the elect may obtain salvation with eternal glory; salvation is an eschatological outcome worth present suffering, drawing the inner being into costly perseverance for others.
Scripture makes one wise for salvation through faith, so the mind's formation in God's word directly serves the inner faculty of faith that grasps eschatological rescue.
The Lord will rescue Paul from every evil and bring him safely into the heavenly kingdom; the inner being rests in confident assurance of final eschatological preservation, evoking praise.
God's grace has appeared bringing salvation to all people; the inner being encounters salvation as something externally given by divine grace — an unearned gift now present and available to all.
The inner being is set in posture of expectant waiting for the glorious appearing of the Savior; hope is the dominant inner faculty, oriented toward future eschatological completion of salvation.
God's goodness and loving kindness appeared as Savior; the inner being encounters salvation as the revelation of divine character — kindness and goodness as the face of rescue.

God saved us not by our righteous deeds but by his mercy through the Spirit's regenerating and renewing work; the inner being is wholly remade — conscience cleansed, self transformed, by unearned divine mercy.
The Spirit is poured out richly through the Savior; the inner being receives the overflow of divine generosity mediated through Christ, connecting Savior-identity to Spirit-gift.

Angels are sent to serve those who will inherit salvation; the inner being's salvation is so significant that celestial ministry is devoted to its beneficiaries — salvation as inheritance shapes identity and dignity.
To neglect so great a salvation is to risk inescapable judgment; the inner being is warned that passive indifference to rescue is itself a fatal turning away, making conscience and will culpable.

Christ is the founder of salvation made perfect through suffering; the inner being is brought to glory by a leader who entered suffering first — salvation is a path through affliction to transformation.

Christ became fully human to serve as a merciful high priest and make propitiation for the people's sins; the inner being's guilt is addressed by a sympathetic mediator whose solidarity enables effective atonement.
Jesus prayed with anguished cries to be saved from death and was heard; the inner being of the Son himself engages in desperate petition — rescue sought through reverence and heard by the Father.
Christ, made perfect, became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey; salvation flows from a perfected mediator and is received by those whose will aligns in obedience.
Those who once tasted the heavenly gift and shared in the Spirit have received real salvation-experience at the inner level — enlightenment and Spirit-participation are the gift's inner content.
The author expresses confidence that his readers possess things belonging to salvation despite the stern warning; the inner being is addressed with assured hope grounded in trust of their trajectory.
Christ saves completely those who come to God through him, because he lives to intercede; the inner being of those saved rests in an unbroken, living advocacy — rescue is perpetual, not merely past.

God promises to be merciful toward iniquities and to remember sins no more; the inner being is freed from the burden of remembered guilt — propitiation produces total forgiveness and conscience cleared.
Christ will appear a second time not to deal with sin but to bring final salvation to those eagerly waiting; the inner being is sustained in active, eager anticipation of eschatological completion.
Noah's faith-driven construction of the ark saved his household from physical destruction; the inner being's reverent faith produces embodied action that delivers those entrusted to him.
The implanted word is able to save the soul; the inner being is rescued through humble, receptive reception of God's word — meekness of will opens the soul to salvation.
Faith without works cannot save; the inner faculty of faith is questioned as genuine if it produces no outward action — true salvation-faith engages the whole person, not only profession.
Only God can both save and destroy, so human judging usurps divine prerogative; salvation is exclusively God's domain, and the inner being is called to humility before the sole Lawgiver-Savior.
The prayer of faith heals the sick and secures forgiveness if there is sin; healing and spiritual salvation converge — the inner being is restored in body and conscience through interceding faith.

Turning a sinner back from wandering saves the soul from death and covers a multitude of sins; the inner being's rescue comes through a relational act of recovery — one person's salvation mediated by another's persistence.

Believers are guarded by God's power through faith for a salvation yet to be revealed; the inner being is held securely in the present toward an eschatological inheritance, with faith the active faculty of perseverance.
The outcome of faith is the salvation of the soul; the soul is the locus of rescue, and faith is the inner faculty through which salvation is presently being received as its telos.
Prophets searched and inquired carefully about this very salvation — its anticipatory longing marks salvation as a grace-gift so great it commanded intense prophetic scrutiny across generations.

Baptism saves not by physical cleansing but as an appeal to God for a good conscience through resurrection; the inner faculty directly engaged is conscience — salvation touches and clears the conscience before God.

Even the righteous are scarcely saved, implying eschatological salvation is achieved only through divine difficulty; the inner being is sober about its own frailty and the seriousness of final rescue.
Faith of equal standing is obtained through the righteousness of God and Savior Jesus Christ; salvation-identity is grounded in divine righteousness given, not earned, equaling believers in standing.
An entrance into the eternal kingdom is richly provided through the Savior; the inner being is assured of lavish eschatological welcome, with salvation as entry into the Savior's eternal kingdom.

Those who once escaped worldly defilement through knowing the Savior but fall back are worse off than before; salvation once known and then abandoned leaves the inner being in greater peril than ignorance.
The Lord's patience in delaying judgment is to be counted as salvation — the time of waiting is itself a saving gift, inviting the inner being to interpret delay as divine mercy rather than absence.
Christ is named as Lord and Savior whose glory endures into eternity; the inner-being call is to grow in grace and knowledge of him, deepening trust and understanding of the one who saves.
Christ himself is the propitiation — the wrath-averting, sin-covering provision — not merely for a limited group but for the whole world, making atonement universally available to the sin-laden.
God's prior love, not human initiative, is the source of propitiation; he sent his Son as the sin-covering sacrifice, so the inner-being receives atonement as pure gift from divine love.
The Father sent the Son as Savior of the entire world — witnessed and testified to — grounding the inner-being's assurance of rescue in apostolic testimony about Christ's universal saving mission.
Salvation is described as shared and communally held ('common salvation'), already delivered, prompting the inner-being to contend and guard what has been given rather than drift from it.
Past rescue from Egypt is recalled as a saving act, but paired with destruction of the unbelieving — the inner-being's believing response is the hinge between rescue and ruin.

Rescue of others from spiritual destruction is urged as urgent snatching from fire, requiring fear and holy revulsion toward sin-contamination — person-to-person rescue with a conscience-engaged moral edge.
The redeemed multitude ascribes salvation entirely to God and the Lamb, expressing corporate joy and praise from those fully delivered — salvation as possession and doxological response.

Salvation arrives in heavenly proclamation at the accuser's defeat; the inner-being of the accused is vindicated as the one who condemns day and night is cast down — accusation silenced, salvation declared.
Heaven's multitude ascribes salvation, glory, and power to God in a hallelujah cry — the inner-being's final doxological response to completed divine deliverance.
